en.extract:
The sooty barbthroat (Threnetes niger) is a hummingbird species in the family Trochilidae. It is found in Brazil and French Guiana.


Taxonomy
The sooty barbthroat was formally described by the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus in 1758 in the tenth edition of his Systema Naturae under the binomial name Trochilus niger. The type locality is Cayenne in French Guiana. The sooty barbthroat is now placed in the genus Threnetes that was introduced by John Gould in 1852.
Two subspecies are generally recognized:

T. n. niger (Linnaeus, 1758)
T. n. loehkeni Grantsau, 1969
The taxonomy and systematics of the sooty/pale-tailed barbthroat complex has caused much confusion. Some authors consider the sooty barbthroat to be a melanistic variant of the pale-tailed barbthroat (T. leucurus). As T. niger was described first, this scientific name is the valid name for both when they are considered conspecific.
The sooty and pale-tailed barbthroats are recognized as separate species by the South American Classification Committee of the American Ornithological Society (SACC), the International Ornithological Committee (IOC), the Clements taxonomy, and BirdLife International's Handbook of the Birds of the World (HBW). However, the assignment of subspecies between them varies. The SACC, IOC, and Clements assign the above two subspecies to the sooty barbthroat. HBW transfers subspecies loehkeni from the sooty to the pale-tailed, leaving the sooty monotypic.
Other authors suggest that the bronze-tailed barbthroat (T. n. loehkeni) should also be considered a valid species and include the doubtful Christina's barbthroat (T. n. christinae) as a subspecies. Freire's sooty barbthroat (T. n. freirei) is intermediate between the sooty and bronze-tailed barbthroats however, and thus its status needs to be clarified before the situation is resolved.
The sooty barbthroat and the other two members of its genus, pale-tailed barbthroat and band-tailed barbthroat (T. ruckeri), form a superspecies.


Description
The sooty barbthroat is 10 to 12 cm (3.9 to 4.7 in) long. The adult male has iridescent bronze-green upperparts and breast, a black ear patch and throat, and a pale malar stripe. The tail of the nominate subspecies is uniformly dark; that of T. n. loehkeni has pale tips to the feathers. The belly is dark brownish gray. It has a nearly straight bill. As with other hermit hummingbirds, the sexes are similar; the female's bill is somewhat more decurved than the male's and the plumage has less contrast among the throat, breast, and belly. Young birds resemble the adult but have ochraceous feather edges.


Distribution and habitat
The nominate T. n. niger is found in French Guiana and the northern part of Brazil's Amapá state. T. n. loehkeni is found south of the nominate in Amapá and Pará states north of the Amazon. It inhabits open and semi-open spaces within humid lowland and higher tropical forest, and other landscapes such as gallery forest, swamp forests, secondary forest, and plantations. In French Guiana it is found from sea level to 500 m (1,600 ft) of elevation.


Behavior


Movement
The sooty barbthroat is presumed to be a year-round resident throughout its range.


Feeding
Little is known about the sooty barbthroat's foraging technique and diet, though it is assumed to be a "trap-line" feeder like other hermit hummingbirds, visiting a circuit of flowering plants. It feeds on nectar at Heliconia and Monotagma plants, and probably several other tubular flowers and also on small arthropods.


Breeding
Nothing is known about the sooty barbthroat's breeding phenology.


Vocalization
The sooty barbthroat's vocalizations have not been described, and as of late 2021 no recordings are available at Xeno-canto or Cornell University's Macaulay Library.


Status
The IUCN has assessed the sooty barbthroat as being of Least Concern, though its population size and trend are unknown. It is "[l]ocal and generally uncommon throughout [its] comparatively small range".


References


External links
Stamps: Threnetes niger (misnamed at site), RangeMap
Sooty Barbthroat photo gallery Archived 2016-03-03 at the Wayback Machine VIREO

The '''sooty barbthroat''' ('''''Threnetes niger''''') is a [[hummingbird]] [[species]] in the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] Trochilidae. It is found in [[Brazil]] and [[French Guiana]].<ref name=IOC11.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/ |title=IOC World Bird List (v 11.2) |last1=Gill |first1= F. |last2=Donsker|first2=D.|last3=Rasmussen |first3=P. |date=July 2021 |access-date=July 14, 2021 }}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
The sooty barbthroat was [[Species description|formally described]] by the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] in 1758 in the [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|tenth edition]] of his ''[[Systema Naturae]]'' under the [[binomial nomenclature|binomial name]] ''Trochilus niger''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=Carl | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1758 | title= Systema Naturae per regna tria naturae, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | volume=1 | edition=10th | page=121 | publisher=Laurentii Salvii | place=Holmiae (Stockholm) | language=Latin | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/727026 }}</ref> The [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] is [[Cayenne]] in [[French Guiana]].<ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1945 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=5 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=6 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480017 }}</ref> The sooty barbthroat is now placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Threnetes]]'' that was introduced by [[John Gould]] in 1852.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Gould | first=John | author-link=John Gould | year=1852 | title=A Monograph of the Trochilidae, or Family of Humming-Birds | volume=1 | location=London | publisher=self | at=Plates 13, 15 and text (Part 4 Plates 14, 15) | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/34843586 }} The 5 volumes were issued in 25 parts between 1849 and 1861. Title pages of all volumes bear the date of 1861.</ref><ref name=IOC11.2 />

Two subspecies are generally recognized:<ref name=IOC11.2 /><ref name=Clements>Clements, J. F., T. S. Schulenberg, M. J. Iliff, S. M. Billerman, T. A. Fredericks, J. A. Gerbracht, D. Lepage, B. L. Sullivan, and C. L. Wood. 2021. The eBird/Clements checklist of Birds of the World: v2021. Downloaded from https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ Retrieved August 25, 2021</ref><ref name=Stotz>{{ cite web | last=Stotz | first=Doug | date=November 2003 | title=Proposal (77) to South American Classification Committee: Split ''Threnetes leucurus'' from ''Threnetes niger'' | publisher=South American Classification Committee, American Ornithologists' Union | url=http://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCprop732.htm | access-date=7 March 2021}}</ref>

* ''T. n. niger'' (Linnaeus, 1758)
* ''T. n. loehkeni'' [[Rolf Grantsau|Grantsau]], 1969

The [[Taxonomy (biology)|taxonomy]] and [[systematics]] of the sooty/pale-tailed barbthroat [[Species complex|complex]] has caused much confusion. Some authors<ref>Hinkelmann, Christoph (1999): 8. Pale-tailed Barbthroat. ''In:'' del Hoyo, Josep; Elliott, Andrew & Sargatal, Jordi (eds.): ''[[Handbook of Birds of the World]]'' (Vol. 5: Barn-owls to Hummingbirds): 539, plate 45. Lynx Edicions, Barcelona. {{ISBN|84-87334-25-3}}</ref> consider the sooty barbthroat to be a [[melanistic]] variant of the [[pale-tailed barbthroat]] (''T. leucurus''). As ''T. niger'' was described first, this [[scientific name]] is the valid name for both when they are considered [[conspecific]].

The sooty and pale-tailed barbthroats are recognized as separate species by the South American Classification Committee of the [[American Ornithological Society]] (SACC), the [[International Ornithological Committee]] (IOC), the [[Clements taxonomy]], and [[BirdLife International]]'s ''[[Handbook of the Birds of the World]]'' (HBW). However, the assignment of subspecies between them varies. The SACC, IOC, and Clements assign the above two subspecies to the sooty barbthroat. HBW transfers subspecies ''loehkeni'' from the sooty to the pale-tailed, leaving the sooty [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC11.2 /><ref name=Clements /><ref name=HBW2020>HBW and BirdLife International (2020) ''Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International digital checklist of the birds of the world'' Version 5. Available at: http://datazone.birdlife.org/userfiles/file/Species/Taxonomy/HBW-BirdLife_Checklist_v5_Dec20.zip [.xls zipped 1 MB] retrieved May 27, 2021</ref><ref name=Stotz /><ref name=SOBA-BOW>Hinkelmann, C., G. M. Kirwan, and P. F. D. Boesman (2020). Sooty Barbthroat (''Threnetes niger''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (S. M. Billerman, B. K. Keeney, P. G. Rodewald, and T. S. Schulenberg, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.soobar1.01 retrieved November 24, 2021</ref>{{overcite|date=March 2025}}

Other authors suggest that the '''bronze-tailed barbthroat''' (''T. n. loehkeni'') should also be considered a valid species and include the doubtful '''Christina's barbthroat''' (''T. n. christinae'') as a subspecies. '''Freire's sooty barbthroat''' (''T. n. freirei'') is intermediate between the sooty and bronze-tailed barbthroats however, and thus its status needs to be clarified before the situation is resolved.<ref>Mallet-Rodrigues, Francisco (2006): Táxons de aves de validade questionável com ocorrência no Brasil. III – Trochilidae (I) [Questionable bird taxa with occurrence in Brazil. III – Trochilidae (I)]. ''Revista Brasileira de Ornitologia'' '''14'''(4): 475-479 [Portuguese with English abstract]. [https://web.archive.org/web/20070927121137/http://www.ararajuba.org.br/sbo/ararajuba/artigos/Volume144/ara144cbro6.pdf PDF fulltext]</ref>

The sooty barbthroat and the other two members of its genus, pale-tailed barbthroat and [[band-tailed barbthroat]] (''T. ruckeri''), form a [[superspecies]].<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, J. F. Pacheco, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 24 August 2021. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved August 24, 2021</ref>

== Description ==
The sooty barbthroat is {{convert|10|to|12|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long. The adult male has iridescent bronze-green upperparts and breast, a black ear patch and throat, and a pale malar stripe. The tail of the nominate subspecies is uniformly dark; that of ''T. n. loehkeni'' has pale tips to the feathers. The belly is dark brownish gray. It has a nearly straight bill. As with other [[hermit (hummingbird)|hermit]] hummingbirds, the sexes are similar; the female's bill is somewhat more decurved than the male's and the plumage has less contrast among the throat, breast, and belly. Young birds resemble the adult but have ochraceous feather edges.<ref name=SOBA-BOW />

== Distribution and habitat ==
The nominate ''T. n. niger'' is found in French Guiana and the northern part of Brazil's [[Amapá]] state. ''T. n. loehkeni'' is found south of the nominate in Amapá and [[Pará]] states north of the Amazon. It inhabits open and semi-open spaces within humid lowland and higher [[tropical forest]], and other landscapes such as [[gallery forest]], swamp forests, [[secondary forest]], and plantations. In French Guiana it is found from sea level to {{convert|500|m|ft|abbr=on}} of elevation.<ref name=SOBA-BOW />

== Behavior ==
=== Movement ===
The sooty barbthroat is presumed to be a year-round resident throughout its range.<ref name=SOBA-BOW />

=== Feeding ===
Little is known about the sooty barbthroat's foraging technique and diet, though it is assumed to be a "[[Trap-lining|trap-line]]" feeder like other hermit hummingbirds, visiting a circuit of flowering plants. It feeds on nectar at ''[[Heliconia]]'' and ''[[Monotagma]]'' plants, and probably several other tubular flowers and also on small [[arthropod]]s.<ref name=SOBA-BOW />

=== Breeding ===
Nothing is known about the sooty barbthroat's breeding [[phenology]].<ref name=SOBA-BOW />

=== Vocalization ===

The sooty barbthroat's vocalizations have not been described, and as of late 2021 no recordings are available at [[Xeno-canto]] or [[Cornell University]]'s [[Macaulay Library]].<ref name=SOBA-BOW /><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Threnetes-niger|title = Sooty Barbthroat (Threnetes niger) :: Xeno-canto}}</ref>

== Status ==
The [[IUCN]] has assessed the sooty barbthroat as being of Least Concern, though its population size and trend are unknown.<ref name=IUCN /> It is "[l]ocal and generally uncommon throughout [its] comparatively small range".<ref name=SOBA-BOW />

zh.extract:
暗色髭喉蜂鸟（台湾作烏髭喉蜂鳥，学名：Threnetes niger），是雨燕目蜂鸟科的一种鸟类。
暗色髭喉蜂鸟体重约5.6克，翼长约58.8毫米，嘴峰长约33.4毫米，喙宽度约2.5毫米，喙厚度约2.9毫米，跗蹠长约4.3毫米，尾长约33.4毫米。暗色髭喉蜂鸟在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在密集的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是植物的花蜜。


亚种
T. n. loehkeni：分布于亚马逊河以北的巴西东北部（阿马帕州）。
T. n. niger：分布于法属圭亚那和邻近的巴西（北阿马帕州）。
